items.headline: City Attorney position to be filled; Baykeeper lawsuit ongoing
items.summary: The City Council will meet in closed session to appoint a new City Attorney. The city is also defending litigation brought by San Francisco Baykeeper in federal court (Case No. 20-CV-00824-EJD, U.S. District Court, Northern District of California), which involves Mountain View and Sunnyvale as defendants.

items.headline: New restaurant 'Ribbit' permitted for University Avenue storefront
items.summary: A new restaurant or food business called Ribbit has been cleared to install an illuminated neon sign at 364 University Avenue in downtown Palo Alto. The permit suggests a new tenant arriving on the city's main commercial corridor.

items.headline: Planning Commission to study artificial turf policy
items.summary: The Planning Commission scheduled a study session on Monday, June 22 to review artificial turf policy. No details on proposed changes or scope were provided in the agenda.

items.headline: ADU wave: Seven accessory units permitted in one week
items.summary: San José issued permits for seven ADUs across different neighborhoods between June 22-23, ranging from $72,961 to $169,090. The cluster includes detached units, garage conversions, and attached JADUs, reflecting continued housing densification through the accessory dwelling unit pathway.

items.headline: Housing Department audits loan portfolio performance
items.summary: The City Council reviewed an audit report on the Housing Department's loan portfolio on June 15. The audit examines how the city manages housing loans, which are a tool for financing affordable housing development and preservation in San José.

items.headline: Sunnyvale to study eliminating chemical pesticides on city property
items.summary: The Sustainability Commission is studying a policy to ban chemical pesticide use on all city-owned or leased land. This affects parks, facilities, and public spaces where residents and children spend time.

items.headline: Artificial turf policy study underway at Sustainability Commission
items.summary: The Sustainability Commission is examining whether Sunnyvale should restrict or change how artificial turf is used on city property. The outcome could affect parks and athletic facilities.
